data.title: Delek targets at least $220M EOP run rate and outlines $520M-$560M 2026 DKL EBITDA amid Iran-driven market disruption
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Delek US Holdings, Inc. (DK) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "I'm extremely pleased with our strong execution in the first quarter" and the quarter reflected "disciplined and successful execution of Big Spring turnaround" plus "continued progress on increasing our free cash flow profile through restructuring of our intermediation agreement and continued success of EOP," according to President, CEO & Director Avigal Soreq.
  • CEO Soreq tied current market conditions to geopolitical disruption: "The events in Iran have created many ripple effects in the markets, resulting in around 10 million barrels of crude production and approximately 5 million barrels per day of refining capacity remaining offline," adding, "We believe the structural product shortage created in this event will continue to impact the market well after the conflict comes to an end."
  • On operations and capital intensity, CEO Soreq said, "Big Spring successfully completed its planned turnaround" and "refinery is running at full capacity," and added, "With no further planned turnaround, we have the highest spending quarter behind us."
  • On the enterprise optimization plan, CEO Soreq said, "We are once again raising our enterprise optimization plan target to at least $220 million on an annual run rate basis" and noted, "During the first quarter of 2026, we estimate approximately $60 million of EOP contribution to our P&L."
  • On DKL and sum-of-the-parts, CEO Soreq said "DKL today reaffirmed its 2026 EBITDA guidance of $520 million to $560 million" and added, "In 2026, on a pro forma basis, with a continued growth in third-party cash flow, we expect DKL third-party EBITDA to exceed 80%" as part of a push toward "our deconsolidation goal."
  • CFO Mark Hobbs reported, "For the first quarter, Delek had a net loss of $201 million or $3.34 per share," while "Adjusted net income was approximately $5 million or $0.08 per share and adjusted EBITDA was approximately $212 million."

Outlook

  • CFO Hobbs gave Q2 throughput guidance by refinery: "72,000 to 77,000 barrels per day for Tyler, 78,000 to 83,000 barrels per day at El Dorado, Big Spring will run 65,000 to 70,000 barrels per day, and lastly, Krotz Springs will run 78,000 to 83,000 barrels per day," with "implied system throughput" of "293,000 to 313,000 barrels per day."
  • CFO Hobbs guided Q2 costs and below-the-line items: operating expenses "between $215 million and $225 million," G&A "between $47 million and $52 million," D&A "between $105 million and $115 million," and net interest expense "between $80 million and $90 million."
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s call framing (Q4 2025), management shifted from a turnaround-in-progress posture ("planned turnaround is progressing well and remains on track," CEO Soreq previously said) to a post-turnaround posture in Q1 2026 ("refinery is running at full capacity," CEO Soreq said).

Financial results

  • CFO Hobbs said Q1 adjusted results included discrete RFS items: "Excluding SREs, adjusted EBITDA and adjusted EPS were approximately $129 million and a loss of $0.98 per share, respectively," and "This removes the impact of our RVO exemption recognition for the first quarter of $82 million."
  • Segment detail highlighted a weak quarter in Supply & Marketing: "Supply and Marketing was a loss of approximately $61 million in the quarter," including "wholesale marketing had a loss of $27.1 million" and "Asphalt contributed a loss of $12.1 million," CFO Hobbs said.
  • Logistics remained a profit center in management’s narrative: "In the Logistics segment, we delivered our best first quarter to date, generating approximately $132 million of adjusted EBITDA," which "includes an approximate $10 million negative impact from winter storm Fern," CFO Hobbs said.
  • Cash flow and spending were positioned around the turnaround year: "Cash flow provided by operations was $461 million" and "Investing activities was a use of $190 million," while Delek standalone capital spending was "$181 million" and DKL investment was "$50 million," CFO Hobbs said.

Q&A

  • Alexa Petrick, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc., Research Division: With Big Spring complete, how should investors think about capital allocation priorities and SRE cash inflow? President, CEO & Director Soreq: "We have a very clear, crisp capital allocation program" and "We see a lot of value in our share price and more to come."
  • Petrick, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc., Research Division: How to think about Q2 captures amid macro volatility? President, CEO & Director Soreq: "we've seen the Strait of Hormuz closed" and "we see steep backwardation"; Executive Vice President of Strategy, Business Development & Investor Relations Bhardwaj: "there will be winners and losers in terms of capture rates" and "we are very well positioned."
  • Manav Gupta, UBS Investment Bank, Research Division: Is U.S. refining advantaged vs. global peers? Executive Vice President Bhardwaj: "U.S. refining will have an advantage" and "the biggest winners will be the guys who have access to barrels even within the U.S."
  • Gupta, UBS Investment Bank, Research Division: Why are SREs more likely/important with rising RINs? President, CEO & Director Soreq: "Risking SREs... will lead to higher price at the pump"; Executive Vice President Bhardwaj: "at $1.50 a gallon blended RIN price, our 2026 RVO compliance is close to $750 million" and "we definitely expect SREs to continue."
  • Matthew Blair, Tudor, Pickering, Holt & Co. Securities, LLC, Research Division: Is Big Spring improving and did the turnaround extend into Q2? President, CEO & Director Soreq: "We are very happy about what we see"; Executive Vice President Bhardwaj: "we are just being a little bit more conservative" on guidance.
  • Jason Gabelman, TD Cowen, Research Division: Outlook for Group 3 vs. Gulf pricing? President, CEO & Director Soreq: "The way we see a group today is actually stronger" and cited "2 pipelines" affecting dynamics "starting second half of this year" and later "3, 4 years down the road."
  • Douglas George Blyth Leggate, Wolfe Research, LLC: What RIN price assumptions and what brings RIN values down? Executive Vice President Bhardwaj: "based upon our current estimates and $1.50 a gallon blended D4, D6, D3 RIN price" Delek shows "a $750 million RVO obligation in 2026," and said "2026 SREs are extremely important to manage 2027 RIN bank."
  • Joseph Laetsch, Morgan Stanley, Research Division: What’s driving repeated EOP target increases and what about further upside? President, CEO & Director Soreq: "We are always looking to how to make it better" and also said, "we are seeing around $600 million to $700 million on a mid-cycle environment of free cash flow."
  • Laetsch, Morgan Stanley, Research Division: Path to deconsolidation/value unlock? President, CEO & Director Soreq: "Deconsolidation is our ultimate goal" and "We see that the current value... needs to have a 7 handle on this unit," while outlining potential paths including "selling assets" and "DKL has the ability to buy on its unit from DK."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative to neutral, with repeated pressure on macro capture and RFS/RIN assumptions, including Leggate’s "the RIN... isn't $1.50, it's $1.90" and timing/scale questions on SREs.
  • Management: slightly positive in prepared remarks and more defensive/firm in RFS exchanges, emphasizing policy framing such as CEO Soreq’s "disproportionate economic harm" and Bhardwaj’s "What exactly EPA will do... they will figure it out."
  • Versus last quarter, Q1 2026 sounded more opportunity-focused on macro disruption and post-turnaround upside, while Q4 2025 emphasized "transformational year" execution and balance-sheet actions.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • EOP target language moved higher: Q4 2025 CEO Soreq said "raising our enterprise optimization plan target to at least $200 million," while Q1 2026 he said "raising... to at least $220 million" with an updated quarterly contribution estimate.
  • Supply & Marketing swung from profit to loss as described by the CFO: Q4 2025 "supply and marketing contributed approximately $23 million" (excluding SREs), while Q1 2026 "Supply and Marketing was a loss of approximately $61 million."
  • DKL separation messaging stayed consistent (both quarters referenced pro forma third-party EBITDA exceeding 80%), while Q1 2026 added operational progress: Q4 2025 "planned turnaround is progressing well" versus Q1 2026 "successfully completed" and "running at full capacity."

Risks and concerns

  • Management highlighted macro volatility tied to geopolitical events: CEO Soreq cited "dislocation between physical and paper grades" and "steep backwardation" as market features affecting outcomes.
  • RFS/RIN exposure remained central in both analyst questions and management framing, with Bhardwaj stating a 2026 view at "$1.50 a gallon blended" RIN pricing and discussing a potential "significant 2027 deficit" in the RIN bank if SRE levels are insufficient.
  • Execution risk was acknowledged indirectly through conservatism around post-turnaround guidance: Bhardwaj said Big Spring guidance reflects being "a little bit more conservative."

Final takeaway

Management presented Q1 2026 as a transition quarter following the Big Spring turnaround, paired with a higher EOP run-rate target of at least $220 million and an emphasis on capturing a disrupted crude/product market environment. In guidance, the company laid out Q2 throughput ranges totaling 293,000–313,000 bpd implied systemwide and cost ranges including $215–$225 million in operating expenses. In Q&A, analysts concentrated on capture rates, Group 3 dynamics, and the sensitivity of earnings and cash flow to RIN prices and SRE timing, while management repeatedly anchored its RFS position on "disproportionate economic harm" and maintained that DKL’s $520–$560 million 2026 EBITDA outlook and the push toward >80% third-party EBITDA remain key steps toward deconsolidation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Garmin maintains 2026 revenue outlook of ~$7.9B while fitness grows 42% in Q1
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Garmin Ltd. (GRMN) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Consolidated revenue increased 14% to $1.75 billion, which is a new first quarter record." (President, CEO & Director Clifton Pemble)
  • "Gross and operating margins expanded to 59.4% and 24.6%, respectively, resulting in record first quarter operating income of $432 million, up 30% year-over-year and pro forma EPS of $2.08, up 29% year-over-year." (President, CEO & Director Pemble)
  • "With this in mind and consistent with our typical practice, we are maintaining the guidance issued in February, and we'll provide updates as the year unfolds." (President, CEO & Director Pemble)
  • "The primary driver of our performance is higher unit volumes, resulting in meaningful market share gains." (President, CEO & Director Pemble)
  • "The year-over-year margin compression was primarily due to higher tariff costs." (President, CEO & Director Pemble)
  • "We continue to achieve important milestones leading up to the launch of our next large-scale program with Mercedes-Benz, which we anticipate will drive significant growth starting in 2027 and beyond." (President, CEO & Director Pemble)
  • "We posted revenue of $1.753 billion for the first quarter, representing a 14% increase year-over-year." (CFO & Treasurer Douglas Boessen)

Outlook

  • "While the initial trends are encouraging, much of the year remains ahead." (President, CEO & Director Clifton Pemble)
  • "Looking forward, we expect second quarter outdoor performance to be similar to that of Q1." (President, CEO & Director Pemble)
  • "We also expect to achieve stronger performance in the back half of the year due to the timing of product launches, resulting in improved full year growth when compared to 2025." (President, CEO & Director Pemble)
  • "We are very pleased with the performance of aviation during the first quarter, and we expect to achieve solid growth throughout the remainder of the year." (President, CEO & Director Pemble)
  • "As a reminder, we expect auto OEM revenue to decrease in 2026 as the BMW program has reached peak volumes and as certain legacy programs approach end of life." (President, CEO & Director Pemble)

Financial Results

  • "Gross margin was 59.4%, a 180 basis point increase from the prior year quarter." (CFO & Treasurer Douglas Boessen)
  • "Operating expense as a percentage of sales was 34.8%, 110 basis point decrease." (CFO & Treasurer Boessen)
  • "We ended the quarter with cash and marketable securities of approximately $4.3 billion." (CFO & Treasurer Boessen)
  • "Inventory increased year-over-year and sequentially to approximately $1.9 billion." (CFO & Treasurer Boessen)
  • "During the first quarter of 2026, we generated free cash flow of $469 million" and "capital expenditures for the first quarter of 2026 were $67 million." (CFO & Treasurer Boessen)
  • "During the first quarter of 2026, we paid dividends of approximately $174 million, purchased $40 million of company stock." (CFO & Treasurer Boessen)

Q&A

  • Joseph Cardoso, JPMorgan Chase & Co, Research Division: "can you just touch on what is reinforcing" demand momentum and component-cost navigation? President, CEO & Director Pemble: "Registration rates are continuing to be strong" and "we are not experiencing" component cost pressure in current results, adding "we do have significant safety stock" and "I would expect that 2026 is still going to be somewhat muted, and we'll start to see some effect in 2027."
  • Joseph Cardoso, JPMorgan Chase & Co, Research Division: asked about private wearable challengers and subscriptions; President, CEO & Director Pemble said "customers want choices" and "we have been expanding our role in subscription-based services" including "Garmin Connect Plus."
  • Alyssa Shreves, Barclays Bank PLC, Research Division: asked about low-tier wearables; President, CEO & Director Pemble said "we're definitely seeing strong demand, both at the low end and the high end" within advanced wearables.
  • David S. MacGregor, Longbow Research LLC: asked whether Q1 was a heavier new-product quarter and the mix impact; President, CEO & Director Pemble said "we typically release somewhere around 100 new products a year" and "our revenue mix from new products tends to be pretty similar from year-to-year."
  • Benjamin Bollin, Cleveland Research Company LLC: asked about aviation OEM deliveries vs retrofit and backlog; President, CEO & Director Pemble said "new deliveries are definitely a strong contributor" and "Aviation aircraft makers are sitting on high backlog still."
  • Noah Zatzkin, KeyBanc Capital Markets Inc., Research Division: asked about tariff policy changes and refunds; CFO & Treasurer Boessen said "we do expect there to be some tariff impact for the remainder of the year" and "we have not recorded any receivable or benefit for those refunds at this point in time."
  • Noah Zatzkin, KeyBanc Capital Markets Inc., Research Division: asked marine trends and share; President, CEO & Director Pemble said "we saw particular strength on deliveries to builders" and cited "a very, very positive reaction to our new products, particularly the Spy pole and the 360 sonar."

Sentiment Analysis

  • "Hoping to get your thoughts" (Noah Zatzkin, KeyBanc Capital Markets Inc., Research Division) and "I was kind of taken aback" (Erik Woodring, Morgan Stanley, Research Division) reflected a slightly negative-to-pressing analyst tone focused on tariffs, input costs, and macro sensitivity.
  • "We're off to a great start in 2026" and "we are maintaining the guidance issued in February" (President, CEO & Director Clifton Pemble) reflected a slightly positive management tone in prepared remarks, while Q&A responses emphasized caution with "much of the year remains ahead" and specificity on cost timing with "we'll start to see some effect in 2027." (President, CEO & Director Pemble)
  • Compared with last quarter’s framing of supply chain risks, the current call used more near-term stability language on demand ("Registration rates are continuing to be strong") while keeping a similar caution on costs and uncertainty. (President, CEO & Director Pemble)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • In Q1, management reiterated guidance discipline without repeating the full-year numbers: "we are maintaining the guidance issued in February." (President, CEO & Director Clifton Pemble)
  • In Q4, management provided explicit 2026 targets: "We expect revenue to increase approximately 9% to $7.9 billion" and "This results in expected pro forma earnings per share approximately $9.35." (President, CEO & Director Clifton Pemble; CFO & Treasurer Douglas Boessen)
  • The segment narrative shifted from Q4’s expectation that "full year growth in Outdoor" would "accelerate in 2026" (President, CEO & Director Pemble) to Q1’s reported Outdoor decline and near-term pacing: "Revenue decreased 5%" and "we expect second quarter outdoor performance to be similar to that of Q1." (President, CEO & Director Pemble)
  • Tariffs moved from Q4’s emphasis on mitigation and scenario planning to Q1’s financial statement impacts and accounting posture: "The year-over-year margin compression was primarily due to higher tariff costs" and "we do not record any benefit or receivable" for refunds. (President, CEO & Director Pemble; CFO & Treasurer Boessen)

Risks and Concerns

  • "We continue to outperform expectations in a business environment characterized by economic whiplash and geopolitical uncertainty." (President, CEO & Director Clifton Pemble)
  • "The year-over-year margin compression was primarily due to higher tariff costs" and management said refunds remain unbooked: "we have not recorded any receivable or benefit for those refunds at this point in time." (President, CEO & Director Pemble; CFO & Treasurer Douglas Boessen)
  • On input costs, management highlighted timing risk: "component costs come through our inventory" and "we'll start to see some effect in 2027," while saying 2026 is "well controlled" via "significant safety stock." (President, CEO & Director Pemble)
  • On end-market sensitivity, management flagged potential hesitancy: "some of those markets like marine and aviation" can see "a little more hesitancy" tied to "oil prices and conflict" and added, for Marine, "customers start to express some worry." (President, CEO & Director Pemble)

Final Takeaway

Garmin’s management described Q1 as a record start driven by wearables-led Fitness growth and expanding consolidated margins, while keeping full-year guidance unchanged and emphasizing that Q1 is seasonally the lowest quarter. In Q&A, executives reinforced steady demand signals via registrations, positioned 2026 component-cost exposure as muted due to safety stock, and framed higher input costs as more visible in 2027. Key watch items remained tariffs (including unrecognized potential refunds), Outdoor’s near-term softness with a back-half launch-led recovery plan, and the Auto OEM transition toward a Mercedes-Benz ramp that management said should drive significant growth starting in 2027 and beyond.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Visa stock up with five blockchains added to stablecoin settlement pilot; CRCL, BLSH, GEMI down
data.description:
Visa (V) is adding five more blockchains to its global stablecoin settlement pilot, expanding its multi-chain settlement capabilities to nine blockchains.

The five new blockchains to be added are Arc, Base, Canton, Polygon, and Tempo.

The program has reached a $7B annualized stablecoin settlement run rate, up 50% since last quarter.

"Expanding our stablecoin settlement pilot program to more blockchains means our partners can choose the networks that best fit their needs, while relying on Visa to provide a common settlement layer across all of them," said Rubail Birwadker, Visa's global head of growth products and strategic partnerships.

Visa (V) was trading 9.21% higher at $337.78 during afternoon trading on Wednesday, Circle Internet Group (CRCL) was 2.12% down to $92.35, Bullish (BLSH) was 8.21% down to $36.55, and Gemini Space Station (GEMI) was 6.04% down to $4.20.

data.title: Molina wins double upgrade at BofA on Medicaid tailwinds
data.description:
Bank of America upgraded Molina Healthcare (MOH) to Buy from Underperform and lifted its price target to $250 from $152 per share on Wednesday, citing a favorable margin outlook for the company’s Medicaid business.

The analyst Kevin Fischbeck argued that Molina’s (MOH) Medicaid margins are likely to bottom out in 2026, with its path to reach target margins “being more a matter of time and math” as states slowly respond to trend/risk pool shifts in 2024/25, raising rates and margins next year.

“The dramatic changes in Medicaid enrollment since 2023 have created negative risk pool shifts, making it difficult for states to forecast costs and trend, resulting in margin pressure for the industry,” the analyst wrote.

Assuming the company’s long-term targets and normalized margins in 2029, Fischbeck expects Molina’s (MOH) earnings per share to improve by more than $30, generating significant upside for the stock over the next three years.

While there remains a large disparity in Medicaid rates/trends and uncertainty in the company’s other business areas, “MOH could conservatively establish a $30 2029 EPS target (vs. $5 in 2026), which, according to Fischbeck, is nearly twice as large as the consensus of $17.32.

data.title: Cognizant targets 16%-16.2% adjusted operating margin in 2026 through Project Leap
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Cognizant (CTSH) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "We delivered a solid first quarter with revenue growth in the upper half of our guidance range, expanded adjusted operating margin and strong bookings growth" (CEO & Director Ravi Kumar S).
  • "Q1 bookings grew 21% year-over-year" and "we signed 7 large deals with TCV of $100 million or greater, including 1 mega deal valued at more than $500 million" (CEO & Director Ravi Kumar S).
  • "We just announced a definitive agreement to acquire Astreya" and "upon closing, we believe Astreya will add a critical layer to our AI Builder technology stack" (CEO & Director Ravi Kumar S).
  • "Market conditions have become more complex since the start of the year, and we expect the impact from heightened macroeconomic uncertainty to persist in the near term" (CEO & Director Ravi Kumar S).
  • "In Q1, revenues of $5.4 billion, grew 3.9% year-over-year in constant currency" (Chief Financial Officer Jatin Dalal).
  • "First quarter adjusted operating margin of 15.6%" and "first quarter adjusted EPS was $1.40" (Chief Financial Officer Dalal).

Outlook

  • "For the second quarter, we expect revenue to grow 3.2% to 4.7% year-over-year in constant currency" and "this includes approximately 150 basis points from our recently completed acquisitions, including a partial quarter contribution from Astreya" (Chief Financial Officer Dalal).
  • "Our full year revenue guidance is unchanged at 4% to 6.5% in constant currency" and "we expect large deal ramps and 2 full quarters of Astreya contribution to be meaningful second half drivers" (Chief Financial Officer Dalal).
  • "The expected savings generated from the program, net of investments are enabling us to raise our 2026 adjusted operating margin guidance range to 16% to 16.2%" (Chief Financial Officer Dalal).
  • "This leads to EPS guidance of $5.63 to $5.77" and "for 2026, we still expect to return approximately $1.6 billion of capital to shareholders" (Chief Financial Officer Dalal).

Financial results

  • "On a trailing 12-month basis, bookings grew 11% and represented a book-to-bill of 1.4" (Chief Financial Officer Dalal).
  • "Q1 gross margin decreased by 80 basis points year-over-year" (Chief Financial Officer Dalal).
  • "First quarter free cash flow was approximately $200 million" and "during the quarter, we returned about $600 million of capital to shareholders through share repurchases and dividends" (Chief Financial Officer Dalal).
  • "We ended the quarter with cash and short-term investments of $1.5 billion or net cash of $949 million" (Chief Financial Officer Dalal).

Q&A

  • Jason Kupferberg, Wells Fargo: asked about bookings mix and the full-year guide assuming improved discretionary spend in 2H; Chief Financial Officer Dalal said, "we don't exactly break out new versus renewal," and added, "the midpoint does assume a little better discretionary environment than what we assume for quarter 2."
  • Jason Kupferberg, Wells Fargo: asked whether AI is driving "irrational" pricing; CEO & Director Kumar said, "we have infused AI into our rate cards now" and "we are able to share the productivity and also keep it for ourselves."
  • James Schneider, Goldman Sachs: asked how token metering affects margins and whether token costs are billed to clients; CEO & Director Kumar said, "token metering is a reality" and described tokenized rate cards including "A3 is autonomous digital labor."
  • James Faucette, Morgan Stanley: asked about acquisition valuations and capital allocation; Chief Financial Officer Dalal said, "we have committed $1.6 billion to be returned to the shareholders" and discussed funding, "we have now used about $600 million" related to Astreya.
  • Tien-Tsin Huang, JPMorgan: asked whether Project Leap is offensive or defensive; CEO & Director Kumar said, "Leap is to make sure that we get there fast" and framed it as "our opportunity to resize our pyramid."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative in probing near-term visibility and pricing dynamics, with questions tied to "softening" discretionary spend and whether competition is becoming "irrational" (Kupferberg, Wells Fargo).
  • Management was slightly positive and repeatedly emphasized control and momentum, including "we remain confident" (Chief Financial Officer Dalal) and "we feel very confident" (CEO & Director Kumar), while acknowledging "heightened macroeconomic uncertainty" (CEO & Director Kumar).
  • Versus the prior quarter’s more celebratory tone around full-year outperformance, the current call used more cautionary macro language while still leaning on bookings and second-half ramp confidence.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Bookings language intensified quarter-over-quarter, shifting from Q4’s "Q4 bookings grew 9%" to Q1’s "Q1 bookings grew 21%" (CEO & Director Kumar).
  • M&A moved from closed/integration messaging around 3Cloud in Q4 to a new transaction, with Q1 announcing the Astreya agreement and positioning it as "a specialist in AI infrastructure build-out" (CEO & Director Kumar).
  • Margin framing changed from Q4’s reported "adjusted operating margin of 16%" to Q1’s "adjusted operating margin of 15.6%" alongside a new restructuring-style program, with Project Leap expected to drive "approximately $200 million to $300 million" of 2026 savings (Chief Financial Officer Dalal).

Risks and concerns

  • "We achieved these results against a softening demand environment" and "clients are appropriately cautious about making large investments" (CEO & Director Kumar).
  • "Policy changes are creating regulatory uncertainty in key areas of Health Sciences" and "trade policy uncertainty and supply chain disruptions remain realities" (Chief Financial Officer Dalal).
  • Project Leap execution risk and cost were outlined as "costs of $230 million to $320 million" (Chief Financial Officer Dalal), with mitigation centered on reinvestment and operating model changes.

Final takeaway

Management described Q1 as a combination of stronger large-deal booking momentum, a more cautious near-term macro backdrop, and a second-half setup tied to large-deal ramps and Astreya contribution, while introducing Project Leap as a cost-savings-and-reinvestment program that management said supports higher 2026 adjusted operating margin guidance and continued capital returns.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Fiverr forecasts $64M-$80M 2026 adjusted EBITDA while advancing upmarket transformation
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Fiverr International Ltd. (FVRR) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Q1 was a solid quarter of execution with both revenue and adjusted EBITDA coming in at the high end of our guidance range" (Co-Founder, CEO & Chairman of the Board Micha Kaufman), while he emphasized a multi-year repositioning: "we are in the early stages of a multiyear journey to reposition Fiverr from a transaction-oriented marketplace into a trusted work platform for complex high-value outcomes."
  • "Projects over $1,000 continue to grow at a strong double-digit rate with clients completing $1,000-plus projects, up 18% year-over-year" (CEO Kaufman), alongside product and infrastructure work: "we are rebuilding our matching infrastructure from the ground up" and "moving from keyword-based matching to context-aware, outcome-driven matching powered by a knowledge graph."
  • "Recent tests in Fiverr Pro show mismatch rates down nearly 10%" (CEO Kaufman), as Fiverr "shift[ed] ranking from optimizing for conversion to optimizing for expected project success and buyer satisfaction" and described early behavioral signals: "we are consistently seeing higher value engagements, leading to stronger repeat behavior."
  • "We delivered a strong first quarter with both top and bottom lines exceeding the midpoint of our guidance" (Chief Financial Officer Esti Dadon), adding: "Revenue was $105.5 million, down 1.6% year-over-year" and "Adjusted EBITDA was $22.6 million, up 16.3% year-over-year and representing an adjusted EBITDA margin of 21%."

Outlook

  • "For the full year 2026, we expect revenue to be in the range of $380 million to $420 million" (CFO Dadon), and "we are raising our full year adjusted EBITDA guidance and now expect it to be in the range of $64 million to $80 million."
  • "For the second quarter of 2026, revenue is expected to be between $95 million to $103 million" (CFO Dadon), and "Adjusted EBITDA is expected to be between $16 million to $20 million."
  • "Looking ahead, macro conditions remain largely unchanged" (CFO Dadon), and "we expect marketplace growth for the remainder of the year and on a full year basis to track broadly in line with Q1 performance," while services growth was described as timing-shifted: "AutoDS ran a successful campaign at the start of the year, pulling certain user sign-ups and revenue forward from Q2 to Q1" and "our expectation for services revenue for this year remain largely unchanged with growth moderation in Q2 and continuing into the second half of the year."

Financial Results

  • "Revenue was $105.5 million" (CFO Dadon), and she attributed the mix as "continued growth in high-value work, offset by headwinds in low-value transactional activity on the marketplace alongside a continued growth of service revenue."
  • "Q1 marketplace revenue was $67.1 million" (CFO Dadon) and she reported operating metrics of "2.9 million active buyers, $356 in spend per buyer and a 27.7% marketplace take rate," adding: "spend per buyer growth of 15% year-over-year."
  • "Service revenue in Q1 was $38.4 million, up 30% year-over-year and accounted for 36% of total revenue" (CFO Dadon), while she noted the quarter included pull-forward dynamics tied to AutoDS.
  • "We generated $21 million in free cash flow in Q1" (CFO Dadon), and on repurchases: "As of March 31, 2026, we had $59.5 million remaining under the current authorization."

Q&A

  • Eric Sheridan, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc.: asked about "the duration of sort of completion of what you call sort of the infrastructure layer" and partner go-to-market; CEO Kaufman replied, "we are anticipating to see results over the remainder of the year" with visibility "more in the second half of the year," and said partner efforts were early: "We're running successful pilots with some initial customers."
  • Jason Helfstein, Oppenheimer & Co.: asked how "agents are evolving" customer behavior and whether new business creation is positive; CEO Kaufman said, "this is amazing news for us" and cited category signals: "AI development up 118% year-over-year."
  • Ronald Josey, Citi: pressed on upmarket talent and matching; CEO Kaufman said, "getting access or getting talent to the platform has never been an issue" but "we're more picky about talent," and framed matching as a moat: "if there's one huge advantage based on data that we've accumulated over 16 years... is being able to take that data and actually make matching like anything that was done before by anyone."
  • Stefanos Crist, Needham: asked for mismatch baselines/revenue impact and the AutoDS pull-forward; CEO Kaufman said, "we haven't publicly shared any specific numbers" on mismatch baselines and called AutoDS a timing shift: "we had a very strong influencer campaign... we were able to actually execute this slightly earlier."
  • Douglas Anmuth, JPMorgan: asked about hiring "AI native personnel" and the EBITDA margin step-down; CEO Kaufman said, "we've added incredible people into the team" and CFO Dadon explained, "that actually reflects the hiring and the investment that we're doing in the transformation."
  • Bradley Erickson, RBC Capital Markets: asked about take rate/pricing changes and marketing intensity; CEO Kaufman said, "there's nothing to call out at the moment" on economics and linked marketing to product trust: "as we create more efficient, higher trust, higher quality solutions... [it will] allow us to also invest more aggressively in marketing."
  • Matthew Condon, Citizens JMP: asked what is driving $1,000-plus momentum and what enables the "end-to-end" platform; CEO Kaufman said, "we're only 2 months into the transformation" and "it's not really about new product launches," while describing work on "an end-to-end fulfilling layer" including "visibility into project progress" and "early detection of risk."
  • Joshua Chan, UBS: asked the upmarket customer profile and buybacks/FCF cadence; CEO Kaufman said, "it's still largely focused on SMBs" and called $1,000-plus "a proxy," while CFO Dadon said, "we plan to continue to generate strong cash flow" and "we will use [the buyback authorization] and act on that thoughtfully over time."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was neutral-to-pressing, with multiple questions probing timing, monetization, and proof points (e.g., Goldman on "when you exit that phase," Needham on mismatch baseline, and RBC on take rate/marketing intensity).
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A, repeatedly stressing early-stage execution (e.g., CEO Kaufman: "it's still early" and "we're only 2 months into the transformation") while maintaining confidence language (e.g., CEO Kaufman: "we are confident in where this leads").
  • Versus the prior quarter’s framing of "We expect tangible impact within 4 to 6 quarters" (CEO Kaufman, Q4 2025), the current call emphasized nearer-term visibility: "we are anticipating to see results over the remainder of the year... more in the second half of the year" (CEO Kaufman, Q1 2026).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Leadership and financial spokesperson changed, with Q4 2025 noting, "today marks his final earnings call as CFO" (CEO Kaufman) and Q1 2026 featuring CFO Dadon delivering guidance and results.
  • Guidance direction on profitability shifted upward quarter-over-quarter: Q4 2025 said "Adjusted EBITDA is expected to be in the range of $60 million to $80 million" (President Ofer Katz), while Q1 2026 said, "we are raising our full year adjusted EBITDA guidance... to be in the range of $64 million to $80 million" (CFO Dadon).
  • The strategic narrative stayed consistent on upmarket focus, but the current call added more operational detail on matching: "mismatch rates down nearly 10%" and the shift to "context-aware" matching (CEO Kaufman), compared with Q4 2025’s higher-level pillar framing of "matching... product... go-to-market... operational excellence" (CEO Kaufman).

Risks and Concerns

  • "Macro conditions remain largely unchanged" and management cited "continued uncertainty in the market conditions" in the Q2 revenue outlook (CFO Dadon).
  • The company flagged timing volatility in services: "pull[ing] certain user sign-ups and revenue forward from Q2 to Q1" (CFO Dadon), and indicated moderation ahead: "growth moderation in Q2 and continuing into the second half of the year" (CFO Dadon).
  • On key transformation KPIs, management resisted quantification: "we haven't publicly shared any specific numbers" on mismatch baselines or impact (CEO Kaufman).

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as execution at the high end of guidance, alongside a multi-year repositioning toward complex, high-value work. The call emphasized early traction in $1,000-plus projects, foundational rebuilding of matching toward context-aware and success-optimized ranking, and an expanding go-to-market approach that includes pilots and partner-led distribution tied to AI-driven workflows. Financially, management maintained the full-year revenue range, raised full-year adjusted EBITDA guidance to $64 million to $80 million, and reiterated disciplined investment in the transformation while continuing buybacks under a remaining $59.5 million authorization.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Smith Douglas homes expects 725-800 Q2 closings while holding pace over price strategy
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Smith Douglas Homes (SDHC) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Smith Douglas Homes reported a demand-and-incentives driven quarter in which management emphasized sales pace, quick cycle times, and expansion. CEO Greg Bennett said the company generated "$4.3 million in pretax income for the quarter" and "net income of $0.06 per share," while delivering "624 homes" and achieving "home closing gross margin" of "19.6% on a GAAP basis." Bennett also highlighted orders momentum, saying, "We generated 981 net new orders, up 28% from a year ago and a new quarterly record for the company," while describing order activity as "choppy" but improving sequentially through the quarter.

  • Bennett framed the operating strategy as prioritizing absorption and backlog quality, stating, "we remain focused on pace over price philosophy," with an emphasis on "driving towards a more presale oriented backlog." He reiterated operational execution, saying, "Our average build time was 57 days during the quarter," and tied competitive positioning to speed and customization: "our ability to deliver homes quickly and reliably with an offering of home choice and personalization" as "a key competitive advantage."

  • CFO Russ Devendorf anchored the quarter with revenue and margin drivers: "We finished the first quarter with $206.4 million in revenue on 624 closings" and "an average sales price of $331,000." He said GAAP gross margin was "19.6%" and adjusted gross margin was "20.3%," adding that margin benefited "by 170 basis points from the reduction of land development accruals on the closeout of several communities."

  • Devendorf also disclosed capital allocation action beginning in the quarter, stating, "During the first quarter, we began executing on our share repurchase authorization," and added, "Including repurchases completed in April, we have repurchased approximately $10 million of stock at an average price of $13.28 per share."

Outlook

  • Devendorf guided Q2 2026 to "closings between 725 and 800 homes," "average sales price between $325,000 and $330,000" and "gross margin between 17% and 17.5%." He said, "Given the continued variability in demand conditions, we are not providing full year guidance at this time."

  • Relative to the prior quarter’s call, management again tied the lack of full-year guidance to macro uncertainty; in Q4 2025, Devendorf used similar language: "Given the continued variability in demand conditions, we are not providing full year guidance at this time." In Q1 2026, he reiterated the same risk framing, saying the "primary risk" remains "macroeconomic conditions, including mortgage rates, consumer confidence and employment trends."

Financial Results

  • Devendorf attributed margin pressure to affordability tools and land basis, saying, "Our margins continue to reflect the use of incentives and targeted pricing adjustments to support affordability and maintain sales pace." He quantified buyer support costs: "closing costs, price discounts and the cost of forward commitments totaled 730 basis points," compared with "430 basis points in the year ago period" and "680 basis points sequentially from the fourth quarter of 2025."

  • On profitability and comparability, Devendorf reiterated the Up-C structure impact and provided adjusted profitability: "For the quarter, adjusted net income was $3.2 million compared to $14.7 million in the same period last year." He also detailed expense intensity: "Selling, general and administrative expenses for the quarter were $35.9 million or approximately 17.4% of revenue," which he said reflected growth-market investment and "the impact of lower average sales price."

  • Balance sheet metrics highlighted conservative leverage and liquidity, with Devendorf stating the company ended the quarter with "$28 million of cash" and "$68.5 million of total debt" and "approximately $195 million available" on the revolver, alongside "debt-to-book capitalization" of "13.6%" and "net debt to net book capitalization" of "8.5%."

Q&A

  • Nisarg Kalra, JPMorgan Chase & Co.: asked for detail on incentives/pricing and construction costs; Executive VP & CFO Devendorf said Q1 margin included "170 basis points" of land development accrual reversals and that "the big driver still for us in kind of margin degradation is the lot cost," adding "lot costs were as a percentage of revenue, it's up about 300 basis points versus last year."

  • Nisarg Kalra, JPMorgan Chase & Co.: asked about early Q2 demand versus March; President, CEO & Vice Chairman Greg Bennett said April traffic was "a slight decline" and described it as "maybe down of 6% to 8% over what we were seeing earlier."

  • Stephen Mea, RBC Capital Markets: asked whether SG&A intensity should moderate as new divisions ramp; Executive VP & CFO Devendorf said SG&A as a percentage of revenue "should definitely start to moderate" as volume ramps in Dallas, Chattanooga, and Gulf Coast, noting "we've got a lot of new fresh G&A that's hitting the books without any volume."

  • Trevor Allinson, Wolfe Research: asked about vertical cost outlook and supplier price increases; President, CEO & Vice Chairman Bennett said, "We've been pretty successful in pushing a lot of those increases off," adding, "the market is not allowing us price" and "we're holding a pretty tough line on that."

  • Trevor Allinson, Wolfe Research: asked about off-balance-sheet lots and land bank terms; Executive VP & CFO Devendorf said "about 30% of our lots under option are with land bankers" and described "about a 10% deposit" with "another 10% walkaway fee" if the company exits, adding, "on all of our new land bank deals, we do not cross-collateralize."

  • Ryan Gilbert, BTIG: asked what drives Q2 margin step-down; Executive VP & CFO Devendorf said incentives are assumed "about flat sequentially" while "lot cost is driving it," with additional variability tied to "how much are we able to hold on vertical costs."

  • Ryan Gilbert, BTIG: asked about current land prices and timing to flow through; Executive VP & CFO Devendorf said land prices are "starting to turn" and "starting to flip from a seller's market to a buyer's market," but added it "takes 18 months to flow through" and that the company does not expect lot-cost pressure "to moderate for at least a couple of years" in a material way.

  • Natalie Kulasekere, Zelman & Associates: asked whether incentives rose into March; Executive VP & CFO Devendorf said the 730 bps largely reflects items sold earlier, but as the company pushed "price discovery," incentives were "up a little bit," while adding the company was "pleasantly surprised" it "wasn't a huge hit."

  • Natalie Kulasekere, Zelman & Associates: asked about spec vs. presale mix and progress toward presales; Executive VP & CFO Devendorf said the business has been about "40, 60 presale versus spec," but also cited improvement selling specs earlier, stating, "we're probably 70%, 80% before drywall stage has got a sale" and "our spec inventory has been coming down."

  • Jay McCanless, Citizens JMP Securities: asked whether smaller markets are seeing stronger demand than large metros; Executive VP & CFO Devendorf said "Alabama has done really well," while President, CEO & Vice Chairman Bennett added, "the in-migration in some of the bigger metro locations we're in is down."

  • Jay McCanless, Citizens JMP Securities: asked about ARM usage; Executive VP & CFO Devendorf said the company shifted marketing to "a 3.99%, 5/1 ARM" from a "4.99%" 30-year fixed and that the "4.99%" is "probably taken the most"; SVP of Accounting & Finance Joe Thomas added, "And we're seeing more usage of the 4.99%."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly negative, with repeated probing on margin durability, incentive intensity, land-cost inflation, and the visibility gap created by no full-year guidance (e.g., Ryan Gilbert asked about the Q2 margin step-down and land price relief timing; Nisarg Kalra pressed on incentives and demand choppiness).

  • Management tone in prepared remarks was slightly positive on execution and demand responsiveness (e.g., President, CEO & Vice Chairman Bennett said, "We are encouraged by the price elasticity we experienced during the quarter"), while Q&A responses were more cautious and constraint-focused on affordability and land basis (e.g., Executive VP & CFO Devendorf: "the big driver still for us in kind of margin degradation is the lot cost").

  • Versus the prior quarter, the cadence sounded more execution-oriented around sales improvement and backlog rebuilding, while maintaining similar caution on macro variability; both quarters used consistent language around week-to-week inconsistency and the decision not to provide full-year guidance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 2026 emphasized a sales rebound and community expansion, with President, CEO & Vice Chairman Bennett highlighting "981 net new orders" and "108 active communities," compared with Q4 2025 commentary that sales conditions "remain choppy" and the company "chose to remain disciplined" amid "aggressive year-end discounting from some competitors."

  • The operating philosophy remained consistent across calls, with both quarters reiterating "pace over price" and willingness to accept near-term margin pressure to protect volume and inventory turns; in Q1 2026, Executive VP & CFO Devendorf reiterated, "our focus remains on maintaining absorption and inventory turns even if that requires some pressure on margins in the short term."

  • Capital allocation moved from intent to execution: in Q4 2025, Devendorf said the company saw "an opportunity to opportunistically repurchase shares"; in Q1 2026, he reported repurchases totaling "approximately $10 million" including April activity.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management repeatedly tied near-term performance to affordability and macro drivers. Executive VP & CFO Devendorf said the market remains "driven primarily by affordability pressures and elevated mortgage rates" and cited uncertainty from "mixed" economic data and "geopolitical developments," adding the company is "monitoring labor market trends closely."

  • Cost structure risks centered on land basis and potential fuel-related impacts. Devendorf said lot costs are the key margin headwind, while President, CEO & Vice Chairman Bennett warned that if higher fuel persists, "we're going to get hit with fuel surcharges."

Final Takeaway

Smith Douglas Homes’ management described Q1 2026 as a pace-driven quarter, pairing record quarterly net orders with continued reliance on financing tools and targeted pricing to address affordability. The company guided Q2 closings to 725-800 with gross margin of 17%-17.5% and reiterated that it will not provide full-year guidance due to variable demand and macro risks. Executives repeatedly pointed to lot-cost inflation as the primary margin headwind, said land pricing is beginning to moderate but will take time to flow through, and disclosed that share repurchases have begun, totaling about $10 million including April.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Apple plans to add Siri mode to camera app and new visual AI tools to iOS 27: report
data.description:
Apple (AAPL) plans to add more artificial intelligence features to the iOS 27 operating system, including a Siri mode inside its camera app along with other new AI visual features, according to Bloomberg.

The company's Visual Intelligence feature will be located within the camera app, the report said. It will appear as a new toggle option. The updates are part of Apple's broader efforts to incorporate AI and will likely be revealed at its Worldwide Developers Conference in June.

These updates to Apple's visual AI capabilities will likely play a role in new devices the company is reportedly working on, such as smart glasses and a pendant. They will allow Siri to scan environments and provide details back to the user.

Apple is also developing an updated version of its Photos app. These include new AI-powered tools for editing and enhancing images, the report said. These will likely be ready for the release of the iPhone 18 series when it's released in September.

data.title: Amazon earnings call preview: AI, tariffs expected to dominate discussion
data.description:
Amazon (AMZN) is scheduled to report first-quarter results after Wednesday’s close, with a conference call set for 5:30 p.m. ET. Prediction market traders on Kalshi are pricing in an earnings discussion heavy on tariffs and artificial intelligence themes.

According to Kalshi data, “tariff” mentions are priced at 96% probability, with “live sports” at 95%. References to “Alexa+ / Alexa Plus” and “silicon” both sit at 94%, while “robotics” comes in at 93%. “Data center” and “Rufus” are each priced at 92%, with “OpenAI” at 90%.

Lower-probability mentions include “supply chain” at 57% and “China” at 32%, suggesting traders view tariff discussion as nearly certain but are less convinced management will explicitly frame pressures around China or logistics specifically.

The prediction market setup effectively maps Amazon’s (AMZN) current investor narrative: tariff exposure in its retail business, AI spending through Amazon Web Services and data centers, in-house chip development, automation and consumer-facing AI products.

When the company posts its quarterly results before the call, investors will be closely watching the performance of its cloud division and looking for signs that the tech giant’s heavy investment in AI has started to deliver tangible results. Wall Street expects the e-commerce giant to post EPS of $1.65 on revenue of $177.16B.

data.title: Verisk reaffirms 2026 adjusted EPS of $7.45-$7.75 while targeting gradual growth improvement after Q1 trough
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Verisk Analytics (VRSK) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Lee Shavel said Verisk delivered “organic constant currency revenue growth of 4.7%” and “sustained strong growth of 7% in subscription revenues,” while “our focus on efficiency and cost discipline drove organic constant currency adjusted EBITDA growth of 5.9%, delivering 60 basis points of margin expansion.” (CEO, President & Director Lee Shavel)

  • Shavel framed near-term headwinds as temporary, saying results included “the carryover impact of the very low weather activity, tougher compares from strong renewals last year and a work stoppage in a federal government contract,” while adding, “we have confidence that the resolution of these short-term factors and continued core growth momentum will result in a gradual improvement in revenue growth as we move through the year.” (CEO, President & Director Shavel)

  • Shavel highlighted strategic emphasis from Investor Day: “drive compounding growth by focusing on 4 key initiatives, specifically strengthening strategic client relationships, expanding our proprietary and contributory data advantage, delivering a steady stream of innovations to the market and expanding networks across our businesses.” (CEO, President & Director Shavel)

  • On product momentum, Shavel said enhanced aerial imagery offerings “growing revenue more than 30% over the last 2 years,” with “wind and hail peril scores and remaining roof life” slated for introduction this year. (CEO, President & Director Shavel)

  • Shavel emphasized anti-fraud AI adoption: “just this quarter, we onboarded the sixth top 10 carrier to the Digital Media Forensics platform.” (CEO, President & Director Shavel)

  • CFO Elizabeth Mann reported GAAP results: “first quarter revenue was $783 million,” “net income was $234 million,” and “diluted GAAP earnings per share were $1.73.” (Executive VP & CFO Elizabeth Mann)

Outlook

  • Mann reaffirmed full-year guidance: “consolidated revenue in the range of $3.19 billion to $3.24 billion,” “adjusted EBITDA… between $1.79 billion and $1.83 billion with adjusted EBITDA margins of 56% to 56.5%,” and “adjusted earnings per share for the year in the range of $7.45 to $7.75.” (Executive VP & CFO Mann)

  • Mann reiterated growth pacing language from last quarter, stating, “we expect the first quarter of 2026 to be a trough, both in terms of organic constant currency revenue growth rates and absolute dollars and forecast a gradual improvement as we move through the year.” (Executive VP & CFO Mann)

  • On comparables and notable items embedded in guidance, Mann said, “we continue to face tougher comparisons in the first half,” guidance reflects the Verisk Marketing Solutions divestiture, which “represents an $0.11 headwind to earnings per share,” and “the prior year quarter’s reported margin benefited from a foreign currency translation impact… 120 basis points… which we do not expect to recur.” (Executive VP & CFO Mann)

Financial Results

  • Mann detailed segment performance on an organic constant currency basis: “OCC revenues grew 4.7% with growth of 5.3% in underwriting and 3.4% in claims,” while “subscription revenues, which comprised 84% of our total revenues in the quarter, grew 7%.” (Executive VP & CFO Mann)

  • Mann described mixed revenue type performance: “transactional revenues… comprised 16% of our total revenues… declined 6.1%,” citing “lower volumes in Property and Restoration Solutions due to low levels of weather activity,” plus “softness in the personal lines auto business.” (Executive VP & CFO Mann)

  • Mann said profitability reflected leverage and investment: “total adjusted EBITDA margins… were 55.9%, up 60 basis points,” driven by “global talent optimization, offset in part by increased investment in AI technology.” (Executive VP & CFO Mann)

  • Mann updated capital structure and interest expense drivers: “net interest expense was $43 million,” and “we issued $1 billion of senior notes and entered into a $500 million term loan” to fund “the… $1.5 billion accelerated share repurchase program,” with leverage at “2.4x debt to adjusted EBITDA.” (Executive VP & CFO Mann)

  • On cash returns, Mann said Verisk paid “a cash dividend of $0.50 per share,” initiated the “$1.5 billion accelerated share repurchase program,” and “in total, we retired 7.6 million shares in the first quarter of ’26.” (Executive VP & CFO Mann)

Q&A

  • Ashish Sabadra, RBC Capital Markets: asked about renewal pricing; CEO Shavel said large-client multiyear renewals achieved “stronger price increases,” with contract terms “averaging approximately between 4 and 5 years,” and annual increases “reflective of the greater value… through our Core Lines reimagine initiative.” (CEO, President & Director Shavel)

  • Toni Kaplan, Morgan Stanley: asked about AI/data monetization; Shavel said monetization is “rooted” in delivering “structured, clean industry-wide” datasets, with strategy including “realizing more value through our pricing arrangements” and enabling “new specific data utilization for AI applications.” (CEO, President & Director Shavel)

  • Jeffrey Meuler, Baird: asked if extended AI sales cycles imply added caution; Shavel said AI adds “additional complexity” around “intellectual property… privacy,” and “yes, there is a little bit of caution,” tied to contract work even as the pipeline grows. (CEO, President & Director Shavel)

  • Keen Fai Tong, Goldman Sachs: asked about cadence of improvement; CFO Mann said “we expected 1Q to be the trough,” and “the second quarter” may remain pressured on an OCC basis due to persisting headwinds, before “the underlying strength and health of the business… reemerge.” (Executive VP & CFO Mann)

  • Kelsey Zhu, Autonomous: asked about AI sizing and margin impact; Mann said, “we haven’t sized it on the top line,” and “for the time being, we see it as a push on the margin,” while noting productivity benefits and continued investment. (Executive VP & CFO Mann)

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to neutral, pressing on durability and execution timing, including “extended sales cycles” (Meuler, Baird) and whether subscription growth in the “high single-digit range” is sustainable (Scott Wurtzel, Wolfe Research).

  • Management tone was slightly positive and occasionally cautious in Q&A, emphasizing confidence while acknowledging friction points: Shavel said “yes, there is a little bit of caution” tied to AI contracting complexity, while Mann reiterated “we expected 1Q to be the trough.” (CEO, President & Director Shavel; Executive VP & CFO Mann)

  • Versus last quarter’s more expansive AI framing, this quarter added more explicit commercialization detail (e.g., “sixth top 10 carrier” onboarded) alongside clearer acknowledgement of contracting delays. (CEO, President & Director Shavel)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance levels and core pacing language were consistent with Q4 2025, with Mann again describing Q1 as “the trough” and reiterating the same 2026 ranges for revenue, adjusted EBITDA, and adjusted EPS. (Executive VP & CFO Mann)

  • Management’s strategic narrative shifted from Q4 portfolio actions (AccuLynx termination and Verisk Marketing Solutions sale) to Q1 execution against Investor Day initiatives and AI commercialization, including a “competitive RFP” win to co-develop “a next-generation digitally native underwriting entity.” (CEO, President & Director Shavel)

  • Analysts’ questions remained heavily AI- and pricing-focused both quarters, but Q1 featured more probing on monetization models and contracting friction, while Q4 included more discussion of transactional revenue recovery and competitive dynamics in personal auto. (CEO, President & Director Shavel; Executive VP & CFO Mann)

Risks and Concerns

  • Shavel and Mann cited near-term headwinds: “very low weather activity,” “tougher compares,” and “a work stoppage in a federal government contract,” with Mann noting transactional pressure from “lower volumes” in property/restoration and “softness” in personal auto. (CEO, President & Director Shavel; Executive VP & CFO Mann)

  • Management flagged AI go-to-market friction as a risk to timing, as Shavel described longer negotiations due to “intellectual property… privacy issues,” and said resolution should improve “over time” as “industry standards… improve.” (CEO, President & Director Shavel)

Final Takeaway

Verisk management described Q1 as a temporary “trough” quarter driven by low weather-related volumes, tougher subscription renewal compares, and a government-contract work stoppage, while reaffirming full-year 2026 targets of $3.19 billion-$3.24 billion revenue and $7.45-$7.75 adjusted EPS. Leadership emphasized pricing realization in multiyear renewals, continued margin discipline alongside AI investment, accelerating AI product adoption (including onboarding a “sixth top 10 carrier” to Digital Media Forensics), and acknowledged that AI governance and IP considerations are extending certain sales cycles even as pipelines and proof-of-concept activity increase.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
